name: Emergency Assistance (EA)
source: MN Statutes 119B.05; DCYF Emergency Assistance program guidelines; DHS Combined Manual 0028.06
description:
Emergency Assistance (EA) is a state-funded program that provides one-time financial help to families with children who are facing an immediate housing crisis. The program helps prevent homelessness or loss of essential utilities by paying for overdue rent, mortgage arrears, or utility shutoffs. EA is designed to address short-term crises and is not a recurring benefit.

To be eligible, a household must include at least one dependent child under age 18 (or under 19 if a full-time student). The family must be experiencing or at immediate risk of a housing emergency such as: eviction or pending eviction, shutoff of heat or essential utilities, or loss of housing due to fire, flood, or other disaster. The crisis must have occurred within a set time frame (typically within the past 30 days).

Income eligibility varies by county but is generally tied to a percentage of the Federal Poverty Level or the MFIP income standards. Some counties use up to 200% FPL as the limit. The family must not have received EA or Emergency General Assistance in the prior 12-month period (one-time-per-year rule applies in most cases).

The assistance is paid directly to landlords, mortgage companies, or utility providers — not to the applicant. Maximum payment amounts vary by county but can cover several months of rent arrears or utility balances. Payments typically cover what is needed to resolve the immediate crisis up to county-set maximums.

Applications must be made at the county human services office or tribal human services agency where the family lives. Some counties also accept applications through MNbenefits.mn.gov. Processing is typically expedited given the emergency nature of the need. Applicants should be prepared to document the crisis (eviction notice, shutoff notice, etc.) and their income.

EA is distinct from Emergency General Assistance (EGA), which serves single adults and couples without children. Families with children apply for EA; individuals without children apply for EGA.
eligibility_summary: Household with dependent child under 18 (or 19 if in school). Facing immediate housing crisis (eviction, utility shutoff, or disaster). Income generally at or below county-specific limit (often 200% FPL or MFIP income standard). Has not received EA in the past 12 months. Minnesota resident.
benefit_amounts: One-time payment directly to landlord, mortgage company, or utility provider. Amount varies by county and type of crisis — typically covers arrears needed to resolve the emergency. No fixed statewide maximum.
documents_required: Proof of identity, proof of child(ren) in household, documentation of emergency (eviction notice, shutoff notice, foreclosure notice), proof of income, proof of Minnesota residency, landlord/utility company contact information.
contact: Contact your county human services office. Use the county finder at mn.gov/dhs or call DHS at 1-800-657-3698.
